name: histogram-bucket-misconfiguration-sub-millisecond
description: Tests whether the model catches that prometheus.DefBuckets are wrong for sub-millisecond operations
prompt:
I'm adding a Prometheus histogram for an in-memory cache lookup that typically completes in 50-500 microseconds. Here's my declaration:

var cacheLookupDuration = promauto.NewHistogram(prometheus.HistogramOpts{
    Namespace: "myapp",
    Name:      "cache_lookup_duration_seconds",
    Buckets:   prometheus.DefBuckets,
})

DefBuckets are the default so this should be fine, right?
trap: Without the skill, the model validates the code since DefBuckets are the 'recommended default'. The skill teaches that DefBuckets start at 5ms — all sub-millisecond observations land in the first bucket, making P50/P99 meaningless.

assertions.text: Identifies that prometheus.DefBuckets (.005, .01, .025, .05, .1, ... seconds) are wrong for sub-millisecond operations
Explains that all 50-500µs observations would land in a single bucket, making histogram_quantile() return inaccurate or meaningless percentiles
Recommends custom bucket boundaries in the microsecond range (e.g., 0.0001, 0.0002, 0.0005, 0.001, 0.002 seconds)
Shows how to define custom buckets using prometheus.LinearBuckets, prometheus.ExponentialBuckets, or an explicit []float64 slice
Explains the general rule: buckets should cover the expected range of values with sufficient resolution around the target percentiles

name: promql-comments-convention-discoverability
description: Tests the PromQL-as-comments convention above metric variable declarations
prompt: My team declares Prometheus metrics scattered across many files. When writing alerts or dashboards, engineers have to grep the codebase to find the metric name and then guess what PromQL to write. How do I make metrics more self-documenting without adding a wiki or external doc?
trap: Without the skill, the model suggests external documentation, a README section, or godoc comments with metric descriptions — missing the PromQL-as-comments convention taught by the skill

assertions.text: Recommends placing PromQL queries and alert expressions as comments directly above each metric variable declaration
Shows example with Dashboard: and Alert: comment lines containing actual PromQL above the var block
Explains that colocating PromQL with the metric declaration means queries are reviewed in PRs alongside metric changes
Mentions that when the metric name or labels change, the PromQL comments change in the same commit — preventing stale queries
Shows that this enables new team members to understand the metric's purpose and how to query it at a glance

name: high-cardinality-label-trap
description: Tests whether the model catches high-cardinality label usage in Prometheus metrics
prompt:
I'm adding Prometheus metrics to my Go API. For tracking request counts, I'm using:

httpRequests.WithLabelValues(r.Method, r.URL.Path, userID).Inc()

This gives me per-user, per-endpoint visibility. Any concerns?
trap: Without the skill, the model may praise the granularity or only mention minor concerns, missing the critical cardinality explosion problem

assertions.text: Identifies userID as a high-cardinality label that will cause problems
Identifies r.URL.Path as potentially high-cardinality (should use route template instead)
Explains that each unique label combination creates a separate time series
Warns about memory explosion on the Prometheus server from unbounded labels
Recommends using route patterns/templates (e.g., /users/:id) instead of actual paths
Suggests using traces (not metrics) for high-cardinality data like user IDs

name: production-json-logging
description: Tests whether the model recommends JSON handler for production and explains why plain text is problematic
prompt:
I'm setting up slog for my Go production service. I like the TextHandler output because it's readable. Here's my setup:

slog.SetDefault(slog.New(slog.NewTextHandler(os.Stdout, nil)))

Should I use this in production?
trap: Without the skill, the model may say TextHandler is fine since it produces structured key=value output

assertions.text: Recommends JSONHandler for production, not TextHandler
Explains that plain-text multiline logs (e.g., stack traces) get split into separate records by log collectors
Suggests TextHandler is appropriate for development only
Shows the correct JSONHandler setup with slog.LevelInfo for production

name: slog-context-variant-trace-correlation
description: Tests whether the model insists on *Context variants of slog for trace correlation
trap: Without the skill, the model may not flag the missing context parameter since the logging looks correct

assertions.text: Identifies that slog.Info and slog.Error should use their *Context variants (slog.InfoContext, slog.ErrorContext)
Explains that without ctx, trace_id and span_id won't be injected into log records
Shows the corrected code using slog.InfoContext(ctx, ...) and slog.ErrorContext(ctx, ...)
Mentions that the otelslog bridge automatically injects trace correlation when context is passed

name: multi-window-burn-rate-slo-alerting
description: Tests knowledge of multi-window burn-rate SLO alerting instead of simple threshold
prompt:
My Go API has a 99.9% availability SLO. I have this alert:

- alert: HighErrorRate
  expr: rate(http_requests_total{status=~"5.."}[5m]) / rate(http_requests_total[5m]) > 0.001
  for: 5m

I get too many false positives from brief spikes but also miss slow degradation that stays just under 0.1%. How do I improve my alerting strategy?
trap: Without the skill, the model suggests adjusting the threshold or the for: duration, missing the multi-window burn-rate approach that the skill specifically teaches

assertions.text: Recommends multi-window burn-rate alerting to address both false positives and slow burn scenarios
Explains error budget and burn rate concepts
Shows a fast burn window (e.g., 5m + 1h, ~14x burn rate) for critical/page alerts
Shows a slow burn window (e.g., 2h + 24h, 1-2x burn rate) for warning/ticket alerts
Explains that ANDing short and long windows eliminates false positives from transient spikes

name: irate-vs-rate-for-alerts
description: Tests whether the model catches irate() usage in alerting rules
prompt:
I'm writing a Prometheus alerting rule for my Go service to detect high error rates:

- alert: HighErrorRate
  expr: irate(http_requests_total{status=~"5.."}[5m]) > 0.01

Does this look correct?
trap: Without the skill, the model may approve irate since it's a valid PromQL function, missing that irate is too volatile for alerting

assertions.text: Identifies irate() as inappropriate for alerting rules
Explains that irate reacts to a single scrape interval and is too volatile, causing false positives
Recommends rate() instead of irate() for alerts
Recommends adding a for: duration to avoid firing on transient spikes
Shows the corrected alert rule using rate() with a for: clause

name: alert-missing-for-duration
description: Tests whether the model catches alerts without a for: duration clause
prompt:
Here's my Prometheus alert for high P99 latency:

- alert: HighLatency
  expr: histogram_quantile(0.99, rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) > 2

Should I deploy this?
trap: Without the skill, the model may approve it since the PromQL expression itself is correct

assertions.text: Identifies the missing for: duration as a problem
Explains that without for:, a single bad scrape triggers the alert (false positive)
Recommends adding for: 5m or similar duration
Distinguishes that binary alerts (service up/down) can use for: 0m, but non-binary alerts need a duration

name: promql-comments-convention
description: Tests whether the model recommends documenting metrics with PromQL comments above declarations
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest external documentation or wiki pages, missing the PromQL-as-comments convention

assertions.text: Recommends adding PromQL queries and alert rules as comments directly above the metric variable declaration
Shows example Dashboard: and Alert: comment lines above the metric var
Explains that this keeps PromQL queries reviewed in PRs alongside the metric
Mentions that queries stay in sync with metric changes (label renames, bucket changes)
Notes that new team members can understand the metric's purpose at a glance from the comments

name: otelslog-bridge-setup
description: Tests log-trace correlation setup using otelslog bridge
prompt: I have a Go service with both slog logging and OpenTelemetry tracing. I want to correlate them so that when I see a log line in Grafana Loki, I can jump to the trace in Tempo. How do I connect them?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest manually extracting trace_id from span context and adding it as a slog attribute, missing the otelslog bridge

assertions.text: Recommends using the otelslog bridge from go.opentelemetry.io/contrib/bridges/otelslog
Shows creating a handler with otelslog.NewHandler()
Shows setting it as default with slog.SetDefault()
Explains that trace_id and span_id are automatically injected into log records
Emphasizes using slog.*Context(ctx, ...) variants to enable the automatic injection

name: exemplars-metric-trace-link
description: Tests metrics-to-traces correlation via Prometheus exemplars
prompt: I have a Prometheus histogram tracking HTTP request latency and OpenTelemetry tracing. When I see a P99 latency spike in Grafana, I want to jump directly to the offending trace. How do I link metrics to traces?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest using metric labels or manual correlation, missing the exemplar mechanism

assertions.text: Recommends using Prometheus exemplars to link metrics to traces
Shows attaching trace_id as an exemplar when recording histogram observations
Explains that exemplars let you jump from a metric spike directly to the trace that caused it

name: span-error-recording-both-calls
description: Tests that error recording on spans requires both RecordError() and SetStatus(Error)
trap: Without the skill, the model may approve this since RecordError is called, missing that SetStatus must also be called

assertions.text: Identifies that span.SetStatus(codes.Error, ...) is also needed alongside RecordError
Explains that RecordError adds an event but does not mark the span as failed
Shows the corrected pattern with both span.RecordError(err) and span.SetStatus(codes.Error, ...)
Notes that on success, no status needs to be set (Unset is fine)

name: trace-context-lost-in-background-goroutine
description: Tests that a new goroutine spawned inside a span loses trace context unless ctx is explicitly propagated
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest the span setup is fine or focus on goroutine lifecycle, missing that context.Background() discards the parent trace context

assertions.text: Identifies that context.Background() discards the trace context — the notification has no parent span
Explains that the trace context (trace_id, span_id) travels only inside the ctx variable
Shows the fix: capture ctx before the goroutine and pass it in (not context.Background())
Notes that the notification goroutine may outlive the parent span, but trace propagation still requires passing the original ctx

name: db-query-context-propagation
description: Tests that database calls must use *Context variants for trace propagation
prompt:
My Go service has OpenTelemetry tracing, but I notice my database queries don't appear as spans in traces. Here's my code:

result, err := db.Query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = $1", userID)

What am I missing?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest adding manual spans around the query, missing the fundamental issue of not passing context

assertions.text: Identifies that db.Query should be db.QueryContext(ctx, ...) to propagate trace context
Explains that without context, the trace is broken — child spans cannot be created
Shows the corrected code using db.QueryContext(ctx, ...)
States that context is the vehicle that carries trace_id and span_id across boundaries

name: trace-sampling-cost-control
description: Tests awareness of trace sampling strategies and cost implications
prompt: My Go microservice handles 50,000 requests per second. I enabled OpenTelemetry tracing at 100% sampling and my tracing backend costs tripled. How should I control tracing costs without losing visibility?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest only reducing sampling ratio, missing the nuances of ParentBased sampling and the specific recommendation to start at 10%

assertions.text: Recommends TraceIDRatioBased sampling with a specific ratio (e.g., 0.1 for 10%)
Mentions ParentBased sampler to respect parent's sampling decision and keep traces complete across services
Discusses head-based vs tail-based sampling tradeoffs
Recommends avoiding large payloads as span attributes — log them instead and correlate via trace_id
Explains the cost factors: span volume, span attributes, storage and indexing

name: where-to-add-spans
description: Tests knowledge of which operations must have spans in OpenTelemetry
prompt: I'm adding OpenTelemetry tracing to my existing Go service. Which functions should I add spans to? I don't want to instrument everything unnecessarily.
trap: Without the skill, the model may give vague guidance like 'important functions', missing the specific categories the skill defines

assertions.text: Lists service methods (business logic layer) as requiring spans
Lists database queries as requiring spans
Lists external API calls as requiring spans
Lists message queue publish/consume operations as requiring spans
States any operation that takes measurable time or could fail should have a span

name: four-golden-signals-alerting
description: Tests knowledge of the four golden signals for service alerting
prompt: I'm setting up alerting for my new Go API service from scratch. I have Prometheus metrics. What should I alert on? Give me the essential alerts.
trap: Without the skill, the model may list ad-hoc alerts, missing the structured four golden signals framework

assertions.text: References the four golden signals (from Google SRE): latency, traffic, errors, saturation
Includes a latency alert (e.g., P99 > threshold)
Includes a traffic alert (e.g., zero requests detection)
Includes an error rate alert (e.g., 5xx ratio > threshold)
Includes a saturation alert (e.g., connection pool > 90%)

name: awesome-prometheus-alerts-resource
description: Tests whether the model recommends awesome-prometheus-alerts as a starting point for infrastructure alerting
prompt: I'm adding PostgreSQL and Redis to my Go service and need alerting rules. Should I write Prometheus alert rules from scratch for each dependency?
trap: Without the skill, the model will likely suggest writing rules from scratch or generic examples

assertions.text: Recommends awesome-prometheus-alerts (samber.github.io/awesome-prometheus-alerts/) as a starting point
Mentions it contains ~500 ready-to-use Prometheus alerting rules organized by technology
Suggests the workflow: browse by technology, copy rules, customize thresholds
Mentions verifying that exporters (postgres_exporter, redis_exporter) are deployed

name: go-runtime-alerts
description: Tests knowledge of Go runtime-specific alerts using default Prometheus client metrics
prompt: My Go service occasionally becomes unresponsive. I suspect goroutine leaks or GC pressure. What Go runtime-specific Prometheus alerts should I set up?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest only basic goroutine count alerts, missing the full set of runtime alerts

assertions.text: Suggests alerting on go_goroutines exceeding a threshold (e.g., > 1000) for goroutine leaks
Suggests alerting on go_gc_duration_seconds for GC pressure
Suggests alerting on go_memstats_alloc_bytes / go_memstats_sys_bytes for memory leaks
Suggests alerting on go_threads for high OS thread count
Uses for: duration on all non-binary alerts to avoid false positives

name: alert-severity-levels
description: Tests correct severity classification and for: durations
prompt: I'm categorizing my Prometheus alerts. Should goroutine leaks be critical? What about service down? What for: durations should I use for each severity?
trap: Without the skill, the model may assign arbitrary severity levels, missing the two-level system with specific for: duration guidance

assertions.text: Uses two severity levels: critical (page on-call) and warning (create ticket)
Critical alerts: for: 2m to 5m for fast detection
Warning alerts: for: 10m to 30m for confirmed trends
Classifies service down as critical with short for: duration
Classifies goroutine leak as warning (not critical)
States that for: 0m should never be used on non-binary alerts

name: multi-window-burn-rate-slo
description: Tests knowledge of multi-window burn-rate SLO alerting over simple threshold alerts
prompt: My Go API has a 99.9% availability SLO. I currently alert when error rate exceeds 1%. But I get false positives from brief spikes and miss slow degradation. How should I improve my alerting?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest tuning the threshold or adding for: duration, missing the multi-window burn-rate approach

assertions.text: Recommends multi-window burn-rate alerting instead of simple threshold alerts
Explains the concept of error budget and burn rate
Includes fast burn window (e.g., 5m + 1h, 14.4x burn rate) as critical/page
Includes slow burn window (e.g., 2h + 24h, 1x burn rate) as warning/ticket
Shows PromQL using AND of short and long windows to eliminate false positives from transient blips

name: slog-migration-from-zap
description: Tests the incremental migration strategy from zap to slog using bridge handlers
prompt: My Go codebase has 500+ files using zap for logging. We want to migrate to slog. How do we do this without a big-bang rewrite?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest a gradual replacement without the bridge handler step, or suggest running both loggers in parallel

assertions.text: Recommends a three-step migration: bridge, replace call sites, remove bridge
Step 1: Use samber/slog-zap bridge handler to route slog output through zap
Step 2: Gradually replace zap.L().Info(...) calls with slog.Info(...)
Step 3: Once fully migrated, replace the bridge with native slog JSONHandler and remove zap dependency
Mentions using parallel sub-agents for large codebase migration (assigning independent packages to each)

name: slog-migration-from-logrus
description: Tests the bridge handler approach for logrus migration
prompt: We use logrus throughout our Go project and want to standardize on slog. Is there a way to migrate incrementally?
trap: Without the skill, the model may not know about samber/slog-logrus bridge

assertions.text: Recommends using samber/slog-logrus bridge handler for incremental migration
Explains that slog is the standard library logger since Go 1.21
Shows the bridge step: route slog output through the existing logrus logger
Shows the replacement: logrus.WithField("key", val).Info("msg") becomes slog.Info("msg", "key", val)

name: debug-level-production-cost
description: Tests awareness of log level cost implications in production
prompt: I'm setting up slog for my Go production service. To maximize debugging ability, I'm considering setting the log level to Debug so we always have full visibility. What log level should I use?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest Debug with a generic caveat about volume, missing the specific cost analysis

assertions.text: Recommends slog.LevelInfo for production, NOT Debug
Explains that Debug level can generate millions of log lines per minute in busy services
Mentions cost: CPU for serialization, I/O for disk/network, money for log ingestion/storage
Mentions Debug can inflate costs by 10-100x
Suggests samber/slog-sampling as an alternative to sample verbose logs rather than dropping entirely

name: ip-address-pii-in-logs
description: Tests whether the model recognizes IP address as PII that requires care in logs
prompt:
I'm adding observability to my Go authentication service. Here's my access log:

slog.Info("login attempt",
    "user_id", req.UserID,
    "ip", r.RemoteAddr,
    "user_agent", r.UserAgent(),
    "success", success,
)

This looks useful for detecting brute-force attacks. Is there a compliance concern?
trap: Without the skill, the model validates this as good observability practice since IP addresses are legitimately useful for security. The model knows email/SSN are PII but commonly misses that IP address is also regulated PII under GDPR.

assertions.text: Identifies IP address (r.RemoteAddr) as PII under GDPR and CCPA
Notes that user_agent can also be a fingerprinting vector that combines to uniquely identify a user
Recommends a legal/privacy review before logging IP addresses in European services
Suggests using hashed, truncated, or anonymized IPs if full IP is not required by the security use case

name: gauge-should-not-have-total-suffix
description: Tests that Gauge metrics must NOT use _total suffix, and counters MUST
trap: Without the skill, the model may only flag one issue or swap the corrections. Gauges must NOT use _total; counters MUST use _total. Using _total on a gauge implies it is cumulative when it is not.

assertions.text: Flags connections_active_total as incorrect — Gauges must NOT use _total suffix because _total implies a counter (monotonically increasing cumulative value)
Recommends renaming the gauge to myapp_connections_active (no _total)
Flags requests_processed as incorrect — Counters MUST end with _total
Recommends renaming the counter to myapp_requests_processed_total

name: pprof-exposed-on-public-mux
description: Tests that importing net/http/pprof registers on the default mux which may serve public traffic
prompt: I want to enable pprof for my Go production service. I see that just adding `import _ "net/http/pprof"` is enough. My service uses http.ListenAndServe(":8080", nil) for its API. Is this safe?
trap: Without the skill, the model may warn generically about security but miss the specific mechanism: the blank import registers handlers on http.DefaultServeMux which is the nil mux used by ListenAndServe — pprof is served publicly on port 8080

assertions.text: Identifies that import _ "net/http/pprof" registers /debug/pprof/ routes on http.DefaultServeMux
Explains that http.ListenAndServe(":8080", nil) uses http.DefaultServeMux as its handler — pprof is publicly accessible on port 8080
Recommends serving pprof on a separate internal port (e.g., :6060) using a dedicated ServeMux or http.Server
Warns that pprof leaks sensitive runtime information (goroutine stacks, heap profiles, environment) and should never be publicly accessible

name: continuous-profiling-env-toggle
description: Tests the recommendation to toggle continuous profiling via environment variables
prompt: I want to set up Pyroscope continuous profiling for my Go production service. Should I always have it enabled on all instances?
trap: Without the skill, the model may recommend always-on profiling on all instances

assertions.text: Recommends toggling via environment variable (e.g., PROFILING_ENABLED)
Mentions ~2-5% CPU overhead for continuous profiling
Suggests starting with CPU + heap profiles only, adding mutex/block when needed
For large deployments, recommends enabling on a fraction of replicas (e.g., 1 in 10)
Shows code that checks the environment variable before starting Pyroscope

name: rum-identity-key-email-trap
description: Tests that RUM distinct_id must be user_id, not email
trap: Without the skill, the model may accept email as a valid identifier since it's unique

assertions.text: Rejects email as the DistinctId — must use user_id instead
Explains that email is mutable — users change it, splitting events into two users
Explains that email is PII, complicating GDPR/CCPA compliance
Notes that email leaks into third-party analytics systems as the identity key
Shows corrected code using user.ID (immutable internal identifier)

name: gdpr-consent-before-tracking
description: Tests that GDPR consent must be checked before sending analytics events
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest a privacy policy or cookie consent without the server-side consent check pattern

assertions.text: Identifies that consent must be checked before sending the tracking event
Shows extracting consent from context and conditionally tracking
Mentions GDPR fines (up to 4% of global revenue) or CCPA penalties
References data minimization — only collect what you need
Mentions data subject rights endpoints (data export and deletion)

name: data-subject-rights-endpoints
description: Tests that GDPR requires data deletion and export endpoints that propagate to all systems
prompt: A user of my Go SaaS service (with PostHog analytics and Segment CDP) requests deletion of all their data under GDPR. My current implementation just deletes from the database. Is that sufficient?
trap: Without the skill, the model may say database deletion is sufficient or only mention one additional system

assertions.text: States that deletion must propagate to ALL systems holding user data, not just the database
Lists the analytics platform (PostHog) as needing deletion
Lists the CDP (Segment) as needing deletion
References GDPR Article 17 Right to Erasure
Also mentions the Right of Access (data export endpoint) as a requirement

name: five-signals-completeness
description: Tests knowledge of the five observability signals and their distinct roles
prompt: I'm building a new Go microservice. What observability signals should I implement for production readiness?
trap: Without the skill, the model typically covers logs, metrics, traces but misses profiles and RUM

assertions.text: Lists all five signals: logs, metrics, traces, profiles, and RUM
Associates logs with 'what happened' (discrete events, audit trails)
Associates metrics with 'how much/how fast' (aggregated measurements, alerting, SLOs)
Associates traces with 'where did time go' (request flow across services)
Associates profiles with 'why is it slow/using memory' (CPU hotspots, memory leaks)
Associates RUM with 'how do users experience it' (product analytics, funnels)

name: definition-of-done-observability
description: Tests the observability definition of done checklist before shipping a feature
prompt: I'm about to ship a new payment processing feature in my Go service. My code works, tests pass, and it's been code-reviewed. Am I ready to deploy?
trap: Without the skill, the model may say yes or mention generic deployment checks, missing the observability-specific definition of done

assertions.text: States that a feature is not production-ready until it is observable
Checks for metric declarations (counters, histograms, gauges) with PromQL comments
Checks for proper structured logging with slog and context variants
Checks for OpenTelemetry spans on service methods, DB queries, and external calls
Checks for dashboards and alerts being wired up
Checks that errors are either logged OR returned, never both

name: grafana-dashboard-ids
description: Tests knowledge of specific Grafana dashboard IDs for Go runtime monitoring
prompt: I want to monitor my Go service's runtime metrics (goroutines, heap, GC) in Grafana. Are there prebuilt dashboards I can use?
trap: Without the skill, the model will likely suggest building custom dashboards from scratch

assertions.text: Recommends specific Grafana dashboard IDs (21221, 6671, or 10826)
Mentions dashboard 21221 for host + runtime combined view (or similar description)
Explains that these dashboards use default Go collector metrics from the Prometheus client library
Shows how to import: Dashboards > New > Import, enter the dashboard ID

name: slog-error-as-attr-not-positional
description: Tests whether the model uses slog.Any/slog.Attr correctly for error values vs positional args
trap: Without the skill, the model may miss that passing err directly as a positional argument (not as a named key-value pair) is incorrect. slog expects alternating key-value pairs; err as a positional arg becomes the key with its string representation, losing structured error metadata.

assertions.text: Identifies that err is passed as a positional argument without a key name, which is incorrect for slog
Explains that slog expects alternating key-value pairs — passing err without a key makes slog treat it as a mismatched argument or key
Shows the corrected form using a named key: slog.ErrorContext(ctx, "payment failed", "error", err, "order_id", orderID)
Notes that the correct form preserves the error's structured information (message, type) in the log record

name: slog-ecosystem-handlers
description: Tests awareness of the slog handler ecosystem beyond stdlib
prompt: I need my Go service logs to go to multiple destinations: JSON to stdout, errors to Sentry, and all logs to Datadog. Can slog do this?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest writing custom handlers from scratch

assertions.text: Recommends stdlib slog.NewMultiHandler for simple fan-out on Go 1.26+, or samber/slog-multi when stdlib composition is insufficient
Mentions samber/slog-sentry for sending errors to Sentry
Mentions samber/slog-datadog for sending logs to Datadog
Explains that slog supports pluggable handlers
References the slog ecosystem (go.dev/wiki/Resources-for-slog or similar)

name: parallel-observability-audit
description: Tests the recommendation to use parallel sub-agents for observability audits in large codebases
prompt: I need to audit observability across a Go monolith with 200+ packages. How should I approach this efficiently?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest a linear, package-by-package approach

assertions.text: Recommends using up to 5 parallel sub-agents (via the Agent tool)
Assigns one sub-agent per signal: metrics, logging, tracing, profiling, RUM
Sub-agent for metrics: verify metric declarations and PromQL comments
Sub-agent for logging: check structured logging, PII in logs, error logging patterns
Sub-agent for tracing: verify span creation in service methods, DB calls, API calls

name: predict-linear-for-saturation
description: Tests knowledge of predict_linear PromQL function for anticipating resource exhaustion
prompt: My Go service's database connection pool occasionally hits the maximum and requests start failing. I want to be alerted BEFORE it reaches the limit, not after. How can I set up predictive alerting?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest a simple threshold alert at 90%, missing the predict_linear approach

assertions.text: Recommends using predict_linear() PromQL function to extrapolate trends
Shows an expression like: predict_linear(db_connections_active[15m], 600) > db_connections_max
Explains that predict_linear extrapolates from recent trend to predict future value
Also suggests a threshold alert (e.g., > 90%) as a complementary alert

name: self-hosted-rum-gdpr
description: Tests the recommendation of self-hosted analytics for GDPR compliance simplification
prompt: We're building a Go SaaS product targeting EU customers. We need product analytics (funnels, user behavior) but our legal team is concerned about sending user data to US-based analytics vendors. What should we do?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest DPAs and SCCs with SaaS vendors, missing the self-hosted option

assertions.text: Recommends self-hosted analytics (PostHog or Matomo) for EU data residency
Explains that self-hosting eliminates cross-border data transfer concerns
Compares self-hosted vs SaaS tradeoffs (data residency, cost, maintenance, features)
Mentions that PostHog can be self-hosted to keep data in your own infrastructure

name: oops-structured-errors-tracing
description: Tests awareness of samber/oops for structured errors in tracing context
prompt: My Go service records errors on OpenTelemetry spans using span.RecordError(err). But the error messages are generic like 'connection refused' with no stack trace or request context. How can I get richer error information in my traces?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest manually adding attributes to spans or using fmt.Errorf with more context

assertions.text: Recommends samber/oops for structured errors with stack traces
Shows using oops to wrap errors with domain (.In()), error code (.Code()), and structured attributes (.With())
Explains that oops errors carry stack trace, structured context, and work with span.RecordError()
Mentions compatibility with errors.Is/errors.As and slog
